blocks.text: Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Jason Jordan joins Brackenridge Civil Group Inc as Design Technician in the Engineering group. It is set down here so that there is no disagreement later about what was said at the beginning. I have written it myself rather than have it typed from notes, because the expectations set out in it are mine and he is entitled to have them in the words I would use.

The work is plan production. Site grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility drawings leave this office to a single standard, and the technician's seat is where that standard is kept: layering, line weights, the title block, the sheet index, and the plotting setup that turns a drawing file into a check print an engineer can mark in red. Jason Jordan comes to us already knowing that work, and knowing it is the reason he was hired rather than someone we would have had to teach it to. On any given job he takes direction on the sheets from the engineer running it. He reports to me.

He will not be handed a sheet of his own for the first several weeks. He will check prints against markups, sit with the engineers long enough to hear what a sheet is meant to say before he is asked to make it say it, and read the standards folder end to end. The office opens at seven-thirty and keeps regular hours. Time is recorded against a job number the day it is worked, not reconstructed on a Friday afternoon. Drawing files belong in the lettered project folder on the share and nowhere else: not on the local machine, not on a diskette in a desk drawer. Where the standards do not answer a question, he is to bring it to me. I wrote most of them, and I will defend them or change them on the spot.

Two matters I will state plainly, because neither is a formality. The first is that a plan set going out under a sealed cover sheet carries this firm's name and my seal, and it is checked before it goes, whatever the schedule is said to require. The second is that if Jason Jordan does not know something, he is to ask before he draws it rather than after. This office is small enough that asking costs nothing. Guessing has a way of costing a great deal, and it never costs it quietly. I am glad to have him here.
